Market Overview

The first quarter ushered in a change of US leadership and with it a change in global equity market dynamics.

Though 2025 started well for risk assets broadly, sour sentiment took hold of US markets in mid-February as concerns mounted about the economic impacts of the Trump administration’s policy priorities.

Meanwhile, non-US markets appeared to be reinvigorated by plans for greater fiscal spending in Germany and China. In contrast with recent US dominance, the MSCI EAFE Index outperformed the S&P 500 Index handily during the first quarter, 6.9% to -4.3%, while US exposure drove a 1.8% decline in the MSCI World Index. In another trend reversal, value outperformed growth across markets.1

Of course, circumstances changed dramatically in the early days of the second quarter, as we will discuss later in this report.

Sentiment Journey

Trump’s election victory in November 2024 initially fueled another leg higher in equities, as markets hoped the campaign trail promises of pro-growth deregulation and stimulative tax policy would bolster earnings for a range of US companies amid steady economic growth and cooling inflation. This optimism began to fade well before Trump took office in late January, however, and the unraveling of the so-called “Trump trade” was underway in earnest by mid-February as it became clear that the new administration’s policy timeline prioritized tariffs and program cuts over stimulative measures. It wasn’t long before domestic stock markets had given back all of their post-election day gains and then some.2

Non-US markets were mostly unfazed, however, buoyed by the prospect of increased fiscal spending. Germany’s new governing coalition spearheaded legislation to permanently exempt defense spending above 1% of GDP from its restrictive constitutional debt brake and created a €500 billion infrastructure fund, allowing the European country with the most fiscal space the flexibility to deploy it.3 Earlier, the annual meeting of China’s parliament emphasized the need to boost consumption in the face of external threats like the escalating trade war with the US.4 This comes on top of plans to increase fiscal support by 1 to 2% of GDP this year.

In the US, hard economic data released in the first quarter continued to show persistent economic growth alongside stubbornly above-target inflation in the US. However, soft indicators appeared to capture the early negative impacts of the new administration’s policy erraticism and aggressive cost cutting. Consumers, for one, have grown decidedly more cautious in the face of the prevailing uncertainty. Consumer confidence as measured by The Conference Board has declined for four consecutive months, and its expectations index—which captures consumers’ short-term outlook for income, business and labor-market conditions—fell to a 12-year low in March and sits well below the threshold that usually signals coming recession.5 A survey from the University of Michigan indicated similarly crumbling sentiment,6 while the New York Fed’s latest consumer data revealed notable pessimism across prospects for household finances, employment, loan delinquencies and credit access.7

Business attitudes have also darkened. On Main Street USA, the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index depicted waning confidence in the economy and high and rising uncertainty in what the future holds.8 Globally, business confidence in the year ahead approached post-Covid lows in March, with US manufacturing Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) slipping to below 50 indicating expectations of contracting activity.9 Perhaps most tellingly, tariffs were mentioned more than 800 times in investor events or conference calls during the first quarter, the highest rate in 15 years.10 With this level of concern evident even before the announcement of Trump’s tariff plan, it seems likely the topic will dominate earnings calls in the weeks ahead.

Mind the Gap

While the environment has changed dramatically in the early days of the second quarter—more on that later—it’s worth noting that the US economy entered the year with an ongoing positive output gap, as well as below-average unemployment and above-average wage growth. A positive output gap indicates the economy is operating above potential, and the resulting inflationary impulse typically invites a cooling response from policymakers. For example, the Federal Reserve’s rate hikes in 2022 and 2023 in response to persistently high inflation narrowed but did not close the output gap. With monetary policy settings currently pretty close to neutral, the persistence of the positive output gap likely can be attributed to expansionary fiscal policy and ongoing deficit spending.11

Persistent deficits have also helped support corporate earnings, in our view, and skewed risk perception in US markets. From World War II through the global financial crisis, the country’s primary fiscal position was more or less in balance through decades of offsetting deficits and surpluses. Since 2009, however, economic cycles have been characterized by deeper primary deficits at the bottom and shallower deficits at the top.12 The level of S&P 500 earnings has more or less kept pace with the mounting pile of debt that has resulted from persistent deficit spending for much of the twenty-first century.13

Which is to say that as government debt goes, so too goes the nominal drift in the economy and corporate participation in it. Assuming that current levels of debt accumulation are needed to support expectations of continued earnings growth, a reacceleration in payroll growth, wage growth and inflation are plausible—though underappreciated—byproducts.

Between a Rock and a Hard Place

While it’s painless to acknowledge the structural deterioration of the country’s fiscal profile, fixing it is not. Easy fiscal policy begets difficult choices: Do nothing to contain spending and increase the risk of inflation, or take action to curb it and increase the risk of recession.

Federal outlays as a percentage of GDP have been chronically high since the onset of Covid-19 and amounted to around 23% in fiscal 2024.14 Dividing federal spending into its three major components— discretionary, net interest and mandatory—can help illuminate the challenges facing policymakers seeking to address the issue.

Discretionary spending comprises nondefense and defense spending and is funded annually through the congressional appropriations process. Nondefense discretionary spending supports a wide range of programs, from education and public health to scientific research and tax collections. While the Trump administration has identified nondefense discretionary spending as the lowest-hanging and most politically expedient fruit to pick, at 3.3% of GDP, it’s a small harvest.

So while the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) makes headlines by taking a chainsaw to these outlays, its ability to impact the deficit and debt is minimal. For example, while a 20% cut to the nondefense discretionary budget would be significant to the programs impacted, it would only amount to about 0.6% of GDP.

On the defense side, spending has shifted markedly lower since the end of the Cold War and in 2024 comprised just 2.9% of GDP, slightly above the all-time low of 2.7% in 1999. Given the shifting alliances amid an increasingly shaky geopolitical landscape, it’s hard to imagine this rate going lower. The Trump administration has urged its allies in the North Atlantic Treaty Organization to increase their defense spending in the face of new and emerging threats, and Secretary of State Rubio has signaled that the US would follow suit.15 Trump himself recently announced that he plans to request $1 trillion for defense in the fiscal 2026 budget, up from about $850 billion in 2025.16

Assuming a unilateral restructuring or voluntary default of US debt is off the table, net interest spending is untouchable. The cost of servicing government debt has climbed rapidly in recent years as sub-2% debt issued during the Covid-19 period matured and was replaced by debt at rates in excess of 4%; this trend is forecast to continue.17 Notably, 2024 represented the first year that net interest spending exceeded defense spending. “Ferguson’s Law” posits that any power that spends more on debt servicing than on defense faces a heightened risk of imperial decline. With the US in violation of this law, there is perhaps some inevitability to the country’s pullback from the global stage.18

With both discretionary and net interest spending unlikely sources of relief, meaningful progress on US debt likely will require entitlement reform and/or higher revenue. The bulk of government outlays— representing 14.1% of GDP in 2024—are directed toward programs like Social Security, Medicare and Medicaid, mandatory commitments whose parameters are enshrined into law. Mustering the necessary political will for changes to programs so broadly popular with voters seems like an insurmountable challenge. Generating additional revenue through higher taxes also appears difficult to reach with Republicans controlling the White House and both chambers of Congress.

Enter tariffs. Charged to importers at the port of entry into the US and mostly passed along to end consumers, tariffs act as a sort of backdoor consumption tax on Americans. Yes, they generate revenue for the federal government, but at the likely cost of higher prices and slower economic activity.

Trump championed the benefits of tariffs throughout his presidential campaign, and he quickly—if with shifting degrees of conviction— slapped new levies on specific countries (notably, Canada, Mexico and China) and industries (steel and aluminum) upon taking office.

Designating April 2 as “Liberation Day,” Trump appeared in the White House’s Rose Garden to introduce a wide-ranging package of tariffs, imposing a baseline 10% charge on all imports globally and steeper rates—referred to as, but not actually, “reciprocal”—on countries deemed to be “bad actors.”19

More extreme than markets seemed to anticipate, the tariff announcement unleashed a rout across risk assets worldwide and a significant spike in volatility. Perhaps of greater concern was the selloff in Treasuries and the US dollar, which may suggest wavering conviction in these assets as reliable “safe havens” during periods of unrest. A week later, via social media and with decidedly less fanfare, Trump announced that while the 10% baseline tariff would continue, the “reciprocal” tariff would be paused for all countries except China, which is now subject to a tariff of 145%.20

Seeking Ballast amid the Chaos

We make a point of acknowledging our inability to forecast the future even in the most placid of times, but Trump’s to and fro approach to trade policy—which thus far has included tariffs, counter-tariffs, counter-counter-tariffs, tariff pauses, etc.—has introduced profound instability to the global order.

Given the lack of both historical comparisons and clarity around many important variables, directionality may be the best one can hope for in a forecast. To that end, the global tariff framework as currently designed likely would raise prices and slow economic activity in the US while weighing on both prices and economic growth abroad. Further, tariffs and their indeterminate impact reflect another investment risk in a world rife with them, the aggregate impact of which suggests a greater likelihood of nonlinear market moves such as those we have seen in recent days.

But rather than making concentrated bets on the direction of markets, we continue to focus on investing in a diversified basket of individual assets that we believe have the potential to demonstrate resilience across multiple states of the world. In certain portfolios, this includes gold, whose resilience year-to-date underscores why we advocate for a strategic exposure to the metal as a potential hedge against adverse market outcomes. Gold also serves as a source of long-term deferred purchasing power, easily convertible to cash when market dislocations produce opportunities to invest in high-quality businesses at prices that have suddenly become much more attractive.

Portfolio Review

Overseas Fund A Shares (without sales charge*) posted a return of 10.92% in first quarter 2025. All regions contributed to performance; developed Europe and North America were the leading contributors while developed Asia excluding Japan and Japan lagged. All equity sectors contributed to performance; materials, consumer staples and financials were the largest contributors while information technology, real estate and health care lagged. The Overseas Fund outperformed the MSCI EAFE Index in the period.

Leading contributors in the First Eagle Overseas Fund this quarter included gold bullion, Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. (BABA)(OTCPK:BABAF), Imperial Oil Limited (IMO), BAE Systems plc (OTCPK:BAESF)(OTCPK:BAESY) and Compagnie Financière Richemont SA (OTCPK:CFRHF)(OTCPK:CFRUY).

Gold continued to serve its role as a potential hedge against adverse market conditions during the quarter, setting a series of new nominal highs as the dollar slumped and real interest rates fell. Sustained geopolitical unrest continued to underpin demand from central banks, as did the potential fallout from a shifting and uncertain global trade framework.

Chinese technology giant Alibaba reported strong earnings during the quarter, reflecting robust e-commerce and cloud operations. With the dominant cloud infrastructure business in China, Alibaba should be positioned to benefit from Chinese investment in generative artificial intelligence (AI). Notably, Apple (AAPL) has selected Alibaba to develop AI features for its phones in China. Alibaba continues to invest in growing its businesses and improving operating efficiencies, even as it returns cash to shareholders through dividends and stock repurchases.

Imperial Oil is a Canadian integrated oil company that is 70% owned by Exxon Mobil (XOM). Record production drove strong earnings and cash flows during the quarter, with higher realizations as increased pipeline capacity narrowed the discount for Canadian producers transporting product to the US Gulf. With low cash-production costs and diversification into downstream markets, Imperial should be positioned to withstand prospectively lower commodity prices. Its concentration of assets across just a few key sites moderates Imperial’s need for reinvestment, and management has demonstrated prudent stewardship of capital—with a strong record of returning cash to shareholders through both buybacks and dividends.

BAE Systems is the largest defense contractor in the UK. The company is well positioned to benefit from prospectively easier fiscal policies in Europe as countries seek to increase their defense spending in the face of uncertain military support from the US. BAE’s long-term government contracts generate recurring revenues and provide cyclical resilience.

Swiss luxury goods company Richemont (OTCPK:CFRHF)(OTCPK:CFRUY)—with maisons that include Cartier and Van Cleef & Arpels—rallied during the quarter. The stock was buoyed by reports of a smaller-than-expected decline in Asia–Pacific even as demand in China—which accounts for one-third of the company’s sales—remained challenging. Double-digit sales growth in the Americas, Europe, Middle East/Africa and Japan also helped. Richemont’s diversified portfolio of storied brands, combined with a robust balance sheet, underpin the company’s secular and cyclical resilience.

The leading detractors in the quarter were Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co., Ltd. Sponsored ADR (TSM), Diageo plc (DEO)(OTCPK:DGEAF), Sodexo SA (OTCPK:SDXOF)(OTCPK:SDXAY), Henkel AG & Co. KGaA Pref (OTCPK:HENKY)(OTCPK:HENOY)(OTCPK:HELKF) and Bakkafrost P/F (OTCPK:BKFKF)(OTCPK:BKFKY).

Taiwan Semiconductor is the world’s largest semiconductor foundry, and includes Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Intel (INTC), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Apple among its clients. As a primary manufacturer of advanced chips used in generative artificial intelligence, the company has seen continued strong sales in this category. However, its stock traded down due to concerns about the potential impact of tariffs on its business. The company has made significant efforts in recent years to diversify its manufacturing base geographically and recently announced plans to increase its manufacturing capacity in the US.

Domiciled in the UK, Diageo is a multinational alcoholic beverage company with a portfolio of brands, including Guiness, Johnnie Walker and Smirnoff. While alcohol sales surged during the lockdown of Covid-19, the subsequent decline of at-home consumption has weighed on the performance of Diageo. Concerns about the impact of tariffs were also a headwind during the quarter, as nearly half of the company’s US sales are imports from Mexico and Canada. We remain constructive on Diageo given its unique portfolio of brands, diverse geographic footprint and focus on premium spirits.

France’s Sodexo provides food services and catering to businesses, government agencies and schools in the US and Europe, with a smaller footprint in Asia. The company reported worse-than-expected results for its most recent quarter and lowered its forward guidance, citing slower growth in its US education segment and a modest decline in its retention rate. We believe Sodexo is a well-managed, highly cash flow generative business with defensive attributes.

German chemical company Henkel maintains leading global positions across both its industrial (adhesives) and consumer businesses (beauty care, laundry and home care). The company is experiencing weakness in both its industrial- and consumer-end markets and provided lower-than-expected sales guidance. During the quarter, the company announced the divestment of its North American private label business as it optimizes its portfolio to focus on businesses with better pricing power.

Bakkafrost is the third-largest fish-farming company in the world. The company operates vertically integrated salmon farms, primarily in the Faroe Islands, spanning from feed and smolt production—the stage in which salmon are ready to transition to the sea—to slaughter and processing. Higher harvest volumes across the industry and weakening consumer demand weighed on both salmon prices

and the shares of Bakkafrost during the quarter, as did concerns about potential tariffs on US imports. In our view, Bakkafrost is well positioned to benefit from longer-term growth in global seafood consumption and is insulated by the structural constraints on salmon farming.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support.

Sincerely,

First Eagle Investments

Risks

All investments involve the risk of loss of principal.

Diversification does not guarantee investment returns and does not eliminate the risk of loss.

There are risks associated with investing in securities of foreign countries, such as erratic market conditions, economic and political instability and fluctuations in currency exchange rates. These risks may be more pronounced with respect to investments in emerging markets. Investment in gold and gold-related investments present certain risks, and returns on gold related investments have traditionally been more volatile than investments in broader equity or debt markets. A principal risk of investing in value stocks is that the price of the security may not approach its anticipated value or may decline in value. “Value” investments, as a category, or entire industries or sectors associated with such investments, may lose favor with investors as compared to those that are more “growth” oriented.

Definitions

Federal funds rate is the interest rate at which depository institutions (banks and credit unions) lend reserve balances to other depository institutions overnight on an uncollateralized basis. Gross domestic product (GDP) measures the value of total economic output in goods and services for an economy.

MSCI World Index (net) measures the performance of large and midcap equities across developed markets countries. A net-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income net of withholding taxes. MSCI EAFE Index (net) measures the performance of large and midcap equities across developed markets countries around the world excluding the US and Canada. A net-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income net of withholding taxes. NFIB Small Business Optimism Index is a widely recognized economic indicator measuring the sentiment and outlook of US small business owners across a variety of areas critical to their operations. A purchasing managers’ index (PMI) measures the growth or expansion of certain segments of the economy. S&P 500 Index (Gross/Total) measures the performance of 500 of the top companies in the leading industries of the US economy and is widely recognized as a proxy for the US market as a whole. A total-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income. Nikkei 225 is a price-weighted index composed of 225 stocks in the Prime Market of the Tokyo Stock Exchange. It is widely recognized as a proxy for the Japanese equity market as a whole. German DAX® Index measures the performance of the 40 largest companies listed on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange that fulfil certain minimum quality and profitability requirements. It is widely recognized as a proxy for the German equity market as a whole. CAC 40® Index is a free-float market capitalization-weighted index that measures the performance of the 40 largest and most actively traded shares listed on Euronext Paris.

Indices are unmanaged and do not incur management fees or other operating expenses. One cannot invest directly in an index.

data.title: Betterware De Mexico: Great Value, Good Growth, Average Quality
data.description:
It has been almost 5 years since the COVID-19 pandemic, during which drastic shifts were expected regarding work and consumer behavior. People were in the house more than they ever expected and started looking into ways to make their habitat more comfortable by pursuing DIY renovations and investing in goods such as organizational and kitchen equipment. Companies like Betterware de Mexico (NYSE: NYSE:BWMX) benefited tremendously from this trend; however, once the pandemic was over, its share plummeted. The question is whether this nosedive has reached its bottom, making the stock an attractive opportunity.

The Business

Betterware is an MLM (Multi-level Marketing) firm that operates in the houseware/home organization and cosmetic industry, following the 2022 acquisition of Jafra. It has over 63,300 distributors and 1.18 million associates across Mexico and some Latin American countries.

Since its foundation in 1995, the company has grown to become Mexico’s number one direct seller in its industry.

Its success has come down to the following factors:

  • Distribution Network: The company’s greatest asset is its vast distribution and associate base network across Mexico. New entrants would find it hard to quickly replicate its success, which has been built on creating a huge network size and loyalty, where representatives who have committed significantly to the brand would have a hard time leaving.
  • Innovation: The company offers monthly catalogs, in each one, they offer a vast collection of in-demand products as well as new ones. This strategy forces the company to be constantly innovating and listening to its clients’ needs to launch new products. While they don’t hold any patents, the company’s first mover advantage and brand recognition have given it an edge.
  • Asset-Light Model: Its business model allows it to earn high operating margins, because it manufactures its products with low-cost suppliers, avoiding spending on high fixed costs and instead redirecting expenses towards design, marketing, and distribution. Betterware avoids the high mark-ups of the retail industry, since it shares a smaller fraction of the revenue with its business associates, allowing for higher margins than classical retailers.
  • Data-Driven: The company has invested in technology platforms, such as its Betterware app and digital catalogs. These tools help optimize pricing, product mix, and salesforce productivity. For instance, using its data network, they have decided on targeted promotions and optimal inventory levels for a given product.
  • Entrepreneurship Mentality: Creating brand associates and offering people the opportunity to earn additional income for just promoting products within their network has appealed to many low-income Mexican households, which sadly make up most of the population.

The company does appear to have a moderate moat, driven by its sales network and low-cost model; however, as discussed in the following section, it has been quickly eroding.

The Industry

Betterware operates in a highly competitive landscape, facing competition from traditional retail players and e-commerce. Its products can be easily found in supermarkets, specialty stores, and online, making the company vulnerable to replacement. However, it does offer a social aspect that competitors don’t offer, where customers enjoy demonstrations and recommendations made in person by associates, although younger generations now feel as comfortable with online reviews.

To better understand the company and its role in the industry, we will analyze Porter’s 5 Forces Diagram:

Competition: High – Mexico is filled with direct selling players like Avon, Cklass, Herbalife, and Mary Kay, all of which fight to recruit and retain sellers. While Betterware is the dominant direct selling player in household solutions, it faces competition from other players like Amazon, IKEA, Walmart and smaller supermarkets.

Threat of New Entrants: Moderate – Starting a direct sales company is relatively easy, but achieving Betterware’s size is difficult. However, the cost to produce and source similar houseware is not prohibitive, and a bigger, deep-pocketed player could quickly establish a new seller’s network, although it would take some time to replicate the company’s success.

Threat of Substitutes: High – consumers can easily replace many of the company’s products, especially with the convenience and often low prices of big retailers and e-commerce platforms. Additionally, new entrepreneurs (Betterware’s associates) have opportunities to earn extra income by venturing into other ventures, such as ride-sharing or content creation.

Bargaining Power of Suppliers: Low – The company sources its products from numerous manufacturers that, if needed, could be easily replaced, as there are ample players capable of manufacturing plastic and electronic household items. Additionally, the company’s high volume gives it ample negotiation leverage. Nonetheless, since most of its products are manufactured abroad, currency fluctuations and supply chain disruptions can affect the cost of its imports.

Bargaining Power of Customers: Low – No single customer is significant, and purchases are relatively low-ticket. This fragmentation gives customers little bargaining power. However, customers can collectively influence the business if they perceive prices to be too high. Moreover, its “true” customer is the distributor, and if, as a whole, they feel that their compensation is inadequate, they can force the company to make their commissions more attractive.

In summary, the company operates in a challenging and competitive industry, where new entrants and products could easily make the company irrelevant, prompting management to be innovative and respond quickly to changes.

The Numbers

Betterware’s strength has been its high revenue growth, growing from 2017 to 2024 at a CAGR of 38.40%, and its EBIT shows a similar tendency, growing at around 32.58%, making it a high-growth company.

As discussed previously, it employs a low CAPEX strategy, which can be seen in its ability to improve its gross margins, attributed to higher sales volumes, productivity improvements in manufacturing, and lower raw materials costs. However, its EBIT and EBITDA margins have come down significantly since the high numbers achieved during the pandemic (28.5% and 26.55% respectively). The pandemic was a huge one-time boost for the company, but to continue driving those amounts of growth, it was needed to continue investing aggressively in promotions, marketing, and sales-force incentives, all of which have pushed down margins to lower levels, albeit still above the sector median.

While growth and margins have fared well, the company is finding it more and more difficult to continue delivering on its growth promise. Two red signs stand out, the first one is that its operating cycle is taking longer from 132 days to 234 days, the main reason being inventory turnover, the company is facing challenges to move its inventory, could be due to poor demand forecasting or less willingness from consumers to buy its products, I lean more on the latter option. Finally, Asser turnover is trending lower, meaning less efficient use or poor investment decision, while it is true that the JAFRA acquisition expanded its asset base, the company is less efficient than it used to be.

Lastly, Betterware has taken on a lot of debt to fund its operations, with the Debt to Capital ratio being on average 73%, a high number, but manageable for the company as its Debt to EBITDA ratio has been in the same period around 1.46x. The purpose of this levered structure can be appreciated in the high ROE the company has, which, as depicted in the picture above, has been around 99.14% on average over the last 5 years.

In summary, the company has seen tremendous growth over the last years, but to keep with that rhythm, sacrifices have been made with margins and hoarding more inventory and expanding its asset base.

The Future

For 2025, management guidance has been high-single digit growth, driven by a healthier Mexican consumer, some stabilization in the Mexican peso, and executing initiatives geared towards pricing adjustment and promotions. However, they’ve noted that there are still ample risks in the environment, specifically related to the trade uncertainties, which could increase their costs and also weaken consumers’ pockets.

Going forward in time, the following catalysts could help the company:

  • International Expansion: Betterware operates mainly in Mexico and the USA, targeting Latin American families, but South America could propel the company even further. They just started entering Guatemala and Ecuador, where, according to management, they have seen encouraging results.
  • Continue Mexican expansion: Even after years of expanding into Mexican territory, their penetration outside the main cities is small. Focusing on smaller cities, and rural areas could support the company’s sales.
  • Successful Product Innovation: While the company mainly focuses on generic everyday products, developing a product that makes the company stand out and makes it visible to more people by going “viral” could help the company achieve more visibility.
  • Raising Disposable Income: Mexico’s economy has many tailwinds that should enhance people’s living standards for the coming decades. Raising incomes should translate into better infrastructure, more housing, and more spending on gadgets, all of which could favor Betterware.
  • M&A Target: If the stock continues to disappoint, it will eventually reach a point of extreme undervaluation, and a large player could step in and acquire the company, benefiting the shareholders with a much-needed change.

All of the above are positive catalysts, but the main cloud over Betterware’s future remains the competitive landscape, as big e-commerce platforms have the pockets to rival the company in terms of discounts and delivery efficiency. Also, Mexico’s internet adoption is growing rapidly, making it easier and more convenient for current and future generations to place orders with competitors.

Valuation

To estimate the company’s fair value, I’ll conduct a DCF valuation and explain each input used. Since Betterware discloses its numbers in Mexican pesos, my analysis will be based on that currency. After I have a target price, I'll convert it to USD.

Revenues: Management has said that they expect to reach high single-digit growth for 2025; therefore, I’ll stick with an 8% growth for the next year. For the next 5 years, I’ll assume the company keeps on growing at 10%, the reason behind that number is that the global retail market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 7.65%, however, that is a USD growth rate and as previously discussed there are many tailwinds for Mexican consumers in the following years, and after years of growing at 30% plus rates I expect that management would at least try to hit double digit growth. After year 5, growth decelerates towards the Mexican 10-year risk-free rate of 6.20%, which is calculated as the Yield on the US 10-year bond plus the inflation differential to get a Peso rate.

EBIT Margins: Margins have significantly come down since the pandemic highs. I do think that given competition and structural changes, it will be difficult for the company to reach the same levels; rather, I assume that at year 10, it will stabilize at 11%, roughly in line with the retail sector average.

Tax Rate: I assume the company reaches the Mexican marginal rate of 30% at year 10.

Reinvestment: The asset-light structure gives the company minimum reinvestment needs, thus, I forecast reinvestment needs as a product of the sales to capital ratio, which will be the same as the global retail industry average of 2.85x.

Cost of Capital: To get the cost of capital, I use the WACC methodology. The industry’s beta is 0.84, considering the company's capital structure, we obtain a levered beta of 1.54. The beta, multiplied by a Mexican ERP of 7.12%, yields a cost of equity of 11.12%. The second component is the debt structure, which, given the company's coverage ratio, has a synthetic rating of Ba1. This results in an approximate cost of 6.25%, adjusted by taxes, becomes 4.37%. Considering the mix of 45% equity and 55% debt the cost of capital is 9.40%, but, this is a USD based cost of capital, to transform it into pesos I add the inflation differential and get a 11.10% figure, which I round to 12% to be more on the conservative side and also due to the illiquidity the stock has given its size.

ROIC: During my forecast period, the marginal ROIC I get from my input is 26.81%, very close to the last 3 years' average. However, in perpetuity, I assume it will drop to 12%, the same as the cost of capital, as I don’t see any competitive advantage that would make the company earn a return higher than its cost of capital in the long run.

After inputting all these numbers, I get an expected Mexican peso price of $177.35, translated at USD/MXN rate of $19.20, the USD fair value price would be $9.23 per share, almost 15% higher than the current prices.

Conclusion

An almost expected 15% price appreciation would be considered by many a Buy target; nonetheless, for me, there are just so many uncertainties in the company, that a 15% price appreciation does not justify an allocation to the stock given the risks. The business model, while innovative at its time, is becoming more and more obsolete. Social media now gives anyone the ability to sell in any part of the world and earn income while doing it. Certainly, there are people, especially older generations, who value the social aspect of the Betterware experience, but the convenience, price, and speed of other competitors could outweigh this benefit.

I do not doubt that Betterware will adapt, but in the process, it will have to sacrifice margins and settle for lower growth as competition is going to be fierce. For me to rate the stock a Buy, I would need to see a clear path and tendency to improve margins. Just to put it into perspective, if the company were to reach margin levels of 12% rather than 11% in the next 10 years, the expected return would be almost 31%. However, challenges remain, and it will be necessary to review next quarter’s results to evaluate the company’s ability to improve its operations and deliver value to shareholders.

data.title: America's Debt Pile
data.description:
People are talking more and more about the unfolding debt crisis in the United States.

The chart that really grabs my mind on this subject is this one, the one on the federal surplus or deficit.

Here it is.

Of course, the picture of the accumulating federal debt is also quite dramatic.

But, it is the first chart that always pushes me to ask every time I see it... "What the hell happened after the year 2000?"

Yes, looking at the chart, we see that the deficits were getting larger and larger in the 1970s and 1980s,

However, starting with the George W. Bush administration in 2001, it just seems that the movement to greater and greater deficits becomes more the thing.

And, it is not a Republican thing. It is a Democratic thing as well.

The scary thing about the chart is that beginning with the "peak" reached with the surplus in 2000, one can set up a pretty steady line connecting the "bottom" of the next swing upwards in 2003.

Then, another cycle with the next "bottom" coming in 2011 followed by another turnaround followed by another peak, and another "bottom" coming around 2021.

The slope of the line connecting these "bottoms" slopes downwards. The scary thing is to think of the next "bottom" coming out beyond 2025 or 2026.

Will this be a real possibility?

The current discussions surrounding the current budget projections seem to indicate the reality that this could actually take place.

The first chart presented above is not one that can give us a lot of confidence.

Talk, therefore, is about the "unsustainability" of the deficit spending and debt levels.

The direction that government budgets are moving certainly brings on the feeling that some kind of financial collapse will be coming...and coming soon.

I would certainly argue the case.

The problem is one of timing.

A financial disturbance is going to happen!

When?

I don't know.

U.S. Treasury securities have been downgraded from a top-of-the-line AAA rating to AA by Moody's. This is some evidence that the "professionals" are getting into the discussion.

But, the U.S. rating is still AA...not too bad...and the rating tells us very little about either magnitude or timing.

Beware....

That is about all we can take from this news.

As mentioned above...the situation in the bond market is not a very good one...the situation in the bond market is going to be around for quite a while and in all likelihood is going to get worse...and historians will eventually get to write about "Great" whatever they call it in the near future.

Note, we are rapidly getting on to the century mark of the events of 1929-1933.

Perhaps the greatest problem that surrounds this situation is that the policymakers do not seem to have any real "program" for how the government is going to move forward.

If anything, the current administration has done little but add greater uncertainty to the whole environment.

The administration proposes...then changes direction...then makes new suggestions...and then alters its ideas once again.

The only really steady force in the current situation is the Federal Reserve and there is constant concern over what the current political administration is going to do if it really feels that the Federal Reserve is going to get in its way.

The Federal Reserve seems very determined to continue the approach it has followed for the past three and a half years, but there is great uncertainty about what is going to happen when the Fed and President Trump really finally find themselves at opposite ends of the battlefield.

But, this situation just adds further uncertainty to the scene.

My position at this time.

First, we are going to have some kind of financial disturbance at some time.

Second, the cause of the financial disturbance is unknown at this time. The timing is totally unknown.

Third, stay loose. One needs to keep investing during this period of time, but one must realize that someday, decisions are going to need to be made very quickly, and without remorse.

Fourth, investors must keep very, very alert. In all likelihood, the break in the market is going to come from some area that is not a highly expected cause or location. This is why it is so important to watch in many different places.

Fifth, don't get caught up in "schemes" where you lose flexibility. In this current world of uncertainty, it is dangerous to find yourself "locked into" ways of acting. Uncertainty means that you don't know or have all the information you need. As a consequence, reactions to market movements require that investors look at all the different situations that arise...differently.

Finally, one needs to keep a positive attitude during times like these.

My final piece of advice is philosophical as much as anything, and this advice applies to debt creators. Keep the use of deficits to a minimum! Maintain a balanced budget if possible.

Three cheers for President Bill Clinton and for his Treasury Secretary Robert Rubin!

data.title: Old Dominion Freight Lines - If You Own Only One Trucking Company, Make It This One
data.description:
Let’s say you want to own a trucking company. A piece of America’s commercial circulatory system. Like trains, they carry out the essential function of bringing stuff from where it is made to where it is needed. Unlike trains, they can use the nation’s highway system for their purpose (without needing to invest in it). There are about twenty listed companies in the trucking industry, how do we go about selecting the best one?

To not bury the lead, readers will know I have a “buy” rating on Old Dominion Freight Lines (NASDAQ:ODFL). I view them as the one and only trucking company worthy of owning (with a long-term perspective), and here I will go through the reasons for that.

What makes them unique:

First, a little background. ODFL is a Less than Truckload (LTL) carrier, which means they focus on servicing clients that need to transport products in a quantity that is not large enough to fill up a dedicated truckload. LTL carriers service small and medium enterprises, for the most part, especially retailers and manufacturers.

Within LTL, there are different players, some focusing on price and trying to get market share by offering an acceptable service at a very low cost and others, like ODFL, focused on service – which in freight means timeliness and carefulness.

Back to the trucking industry:

One way to quickly filter out our trucking industry is by recognizing the difference in truckload and LTL in terms of what it means to be one or the other. To be fair, some companies offer both types of services, and these two types of services sometimes compete with one another. Shippers may adjust one way or the other based on what is going on with freight rates.

I decided to exclude everything that is partially of fully truckload. The main reason is the lower barriers to entry that exist in TL. All you really need to do it is a truck and the right driver’s license. Not so for LTL. For LTL you need a network.

For any service area, you need points of collection and delivery (service centers) which require real estate near highways and near population centers. Connecting with the service centers you have your fleet of tractors and trailers, some of do highway duty and others engage in distribution and XXXX.

Over time, winning companies in LTL have the advantage of network effects. Each new service center adds greater value to the system as a whole, increasing the utility of the network and also its economic returns by adding load and thus density to the network. At enough scale, the network is further strengthened by distribution and break-load centers where route can consolidate.

In short, focusing on LTL we add a margin of safety to our group that comes from a higher barrier to entry. The fact that it takes substantial time and capital to build up the network to begin with is a layer of protection to investors in LTL vs. other easier to entry forms of the business.

The LTL group:

Following Yellow’s bankruptcy in 2023, we now have six publicly traded LTL players (ArcBest, XPO, SAIA, TForce, Old Dominion and FedEx Freight). ODLF estimates that public companies represent about 2/3 of the LTL market, which is a significant slice.

For this group, already at a cumulative +$50B, we can see its regarded by the market as a significant value-added activity for the economy, which it is, and most trade at premiums to net asset values; pointing to the recognition of network effects (intangible, but real). The Yellow debacle did not throw egg in the industry’s face. It was more an isolated story of mismanagement and poor union relations (also mismanaged).

FedEx Freight is expected to go public via spin-off next year. Their $10B LTL operation revenues will make it the largest dedicated LTL in the nation, behind Old Dominion.

Out of this group, we next look for who is being rewarded by the market with above average growth. Which companies are winning market share, which is the key to building efficient networks – a benefit more powerful than scale by itself.

Both SAIA and ODLF are the only two companies that have consistently gained market share in the last then years. Either measured by service center count or daily shipping, they have grown where the rest has stagnated or shrunk.

Measured by daily shipping, both have grown at almost identical near 3% CAGR from 2014. Such statistic is volume only, because prices have also increased every year, the actual revenue growth is much faster.

From the above, we have whittled down the task from twenty initially to six LTL companies and finally down to two. Which can be described as the only two consistent market share growers in the LTL business.

SAIA vs. Old Dominion

It is a good idea to start with valuation. If both companies are growing at similar rates, it is natural to gravitate toward the cheaper option in terms of multiples to earnings:

But stopping there would be, in my opinion, a mistake. It always pays to dig a little deeper. The strategies are not the same. Even if volume is growing at the same rate, which means market shares are expanding at similar pace, are the economics (operating ratios, gross margins, etc.) also the same? The answer is: no, not really.

The reason is that each is growing at different ends of the “value” spectrum. SAIA focuses on price, with less emphasis on timeliness and care. ODFL, goes the other way. They seek to sell a premium service for clients needing on-time and quality handling.

With this at the center of their strategy, the company has been named the No.1 National LTL carrier for 15 consecutive years by the Mastio's customer service survey. By this point their service performance is basically unbeatable, with on-time and claim statistics bordering the limits of the physically possible.

Being able to charge higher prices in exchange for higher service levels matters. It is a differentiator that can only be earned thorough a long track record of superior performance (it can’t be faked).

Remember the clients of LTL (small to medium sized manufacturers and retailers), the payback on reliable service is tangible: being able to manage inventories closer to "just-in-time", and higher reliability on production lines and in-store placement.

The fact is that clients do value superior service and show it by a willingness to pay a higher rate vs competitors. And for our purposes, that helps explain the gap in returns between SAIA and ODFL:

And there it is. We cannot stop at relative valuation. On that basis ODFL is about 10% more expensive. Is the business 10% better? Based on returns, ODLF has achieved far superior performance and the gap is widening, with current returns of 27% almost doubling Saia's (respectable) 14%.

Risks

The main risk is in the multiple to earnings. ODFL is not trading "rich" to historical multiples, but +30 p/e is still a hefty level vs. the market. I think it is well worth it (just look at the ROE), but in a recession their business is directly impacted and the valuation could also temporarily suffer.

Over time, and sometimes accelerated by recessions, their market share gains are set to continue. They carry negligible debt and have demonstrated the ability to take advantage of competitor distress.

A risk that seems far away now, is unionization. As the Yellow case proves, union carriers have much greater challenges. ODFL places great care in going the extra mile for their workforce. Some examples are cross training (improving individual resiliency), in-house driver training programs (at no cost), safety bonuses, widely distributed share grants etc. However, nothing ensures their non-union status will remain so forever.

Conclusion

If you have two similarly rated 30-year bonds, one that pays a 4% coupon and another that pays 8%, you would be willing to pay much more, perhaps twice as much for the higher coupon issue. The same applies to equities, only amplified by the fact that unlike coupons, a large portion of earnings is reinvested at the rate of return.

Using the bond analogy, we should be happy to pay a slightly higher price for a 2x "return on equity coupon". Leaving ODFL as the clear winner in the trucking industry, and perhaps the only true compounder.

data.title: China's Looming Liquidity Trap Will Have Enormous Consequences
data.description:
NEW YORK (June 3) - We wrote recently about how the tariff war between the United States and China is unlikely to be resolved, notwithstanding the current "pause".

That's because the tariff dispute has less to do with trade and economics and more to do with geopolitical posturing between two adversarial powers: the incumbent power, the United States, and the rising power, China. We wrote about the mutual political risks from the trade war and the adverse political fortunes both nation's principals could face if the tariff situation deteriorated:

  • For Xi, layoffs and strikes leading to more widespread social disquiet in China. There are already widespread strikes in China, according to China's own government-controlled labor monitor.
  • For Trump, galloping inflation could cause a loss of GOP control of the House in the 2026 midterms to a Democrat House caucus, largely comprised of the same Representatives who had impeached him, twice, in his prior presidential term.

But the path for Xi and China seems to be more perilous: we foresee a liquidity trap leading to long-term economic stagnation, similar to that which was suffered by Japan in its "Lost Decade" from about 1991 to 2001.

In a liquidity trap, monetary policy becomes impotent because consumers tend to hoard money instead of spending it, even if credit rates are low. What's more, because rates are low, lenders tend to be less likely to lend and to hoard cash themselves rather than invest it. As consumption falls, prices fall in sellers' hopes of finding buyers. Theoretically, at least, a liquidity trap ends up in a long-term death spiral in which economic activity simply grinds to a halt: buyers stop buying; sellers stop selling.

China's looming liquidity trap is becoming more and more evident. The last two years, China inflation has not even achieved 0.25%. This chart of China's monthly Consumer Price Index for the last five years shows the CPI has turned negative - to deflation.

The World Bank estimates China's unemployment rate in 2024, before tariffs commenced, to be 4.6%. China's National Bureau of Statistics showed an unemployment print of 5.1% for April. Youth unemployment, for people 16 to 24, is substantially higher, with the South China Morning Post (SCMP) reporting a rate of 15.1% in April. The high rate of unemployment has created anxiety among many recent Chinese college graduates, who fear long delays in being able to commence their careers.

China's domestic producers are reportedly facing stiff challenges trying to sell Chinese products to the domestic Chinese market and are targeting the European market instead. But as we discussed in our discussion of the tariff pause, Europe will resist serving as the "shock absorber" of China's excess production.

In the United States, China's largest market, the SCMP reported exclusively that US retailers are pushing Chinese suppliers to shoulder up to two-thirds of the tariff costs, even as the retailers work to re-source production to other low-cost production centers outside China.

All these conditions virtually assure China's continued deflation and, thereafter, a liquidity trap. This prognostication is supported by two charts from the most recent Peoples' Bank of China survey of depositors, from 2024Q4, from before the President Trump imposed tariffs.

China's consumers have kept spending relatively stable, but a chart of Chinese depositors willingness to consume shows the money hoarders expected in a liquidity trap are emerging. The Chinese are saving more, investing less and consuming more, but mostly on things like education, healthcare, and tourism, not the consumer goods China needs to keep workers in its export goods manufacturing facilities employed.

China can and will attempt to counter the liquidity trap.

China has already launched fiscal stimulus to foster domestic consumption. But adding spending in so in a contracting economy is usually a fool's errand. It merely further weakens the currency.

A move beyond the zero bound?

In monetary policy, the Bank of China's e-CNY, China's Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC) is operational in at least 25 Chinese cities, as of early last month, according to GoldenPi, the Indian fixed-income investment site. The e-CNY is at par with the regular yuan, so its value cannot be controlled right now. But there is nothing to stop the PBOC from withdrawing physical currency from circulation and exchanging it for e-CNY to make it China's exclusive legal tender. (India did this with its two largest currency notes in 2016.) This would expedite China's plan to make the e-CNY its standard currency. Such a move would allow for the BOC to go below the zero lower bound to impose "negative interest rates" to counter the liquidity trap. This would cause the PBOC to engage in a systemic reduction in the value of the e-CNY held by the public to encourage the public to spend, rather than hoard cash. This would cause the signature consumer behavior of a liquidity trap - hoarding cash - to carry a cost.

But slashing the value of the e-CNY to impose an interest rate below the zero bound has obvious political consequences. It would slow - or even prevent - adoption of the e-CNY as an international payment platform, a long-term goal of the PRC. (In the USA, devaluation plus government surveillance of spending are the principal objections to a digital USD CBDC).

SUMMARY

In the short term, President Trump's China tariffs will raise the prices of Chinese goods for US consumers, but that will likely not last long; maybe until the end of 2025 or the first half of 2026.

In the meantime, China's deflationary trend will accelerate, causing the prices of Chinese goods to fall so precipitously relative to the prices American paid before the tariffs were applied. By the end of 2026, US consumers will barely notice a tariff has been applied.

In the meantime, China will suffer a slowly imploding economy, characterized by deflation, unemployment, and growing civil discord, especially among China's young people.

____________________________________

Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe to be under-performing. While we have never written about any companies that are our clients, and do not include companies we write about here in our personal investment portfolios, we do provide here strategies that we would have recommended were the companies our clients. Others discuss new management strategies of non-client companies we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions regarding such companies here, however, assume the company will not change).

Market in Review

January saw an eventful start to the year with devastating wildfires in California, the emergence of China’s DeepSeek as an artificial intelligence (“AI”) competitor, and the beginning of President Trump’s second term, which jumped out of the gates with a wave of executive orders. Risk assets held in well through mid-February amid these developments, but intensifying tariff discussions and weaker economic growth projections drove stock prices down and bond spreads wider in the second half of the quarter. While inflation data suggested progress toward the U.S. Federal Reserve’s (“Fed”) 2% target — falling from 3% in January to 2.8% in February — market participants began anticipating a resurgence in inflation in the near future. The expectation of persistent price pressures was one driver of declining consumer sentiment, which dropped throughout the quarter to its lowest level since 2022. The Fed repeatedly highlighted the uncertain impact of tariffs on the economy, and it chose to keep interest rates at 4.25%–4.50% at both of its Federal Open Market Committee meetings during the quarter. The Fed’s updated dot plot indicated no change to its median forecast of two interest rate cuts in 2025. Front-end rates remained broadly unchanged during the quarter, while the belly of the curve saw yields decline, led by the 5-year tenor, which dropped by 0.43%. The curve inversion in the front end returned, with the spread between the 3-month and 5-year Treasury yields dropping from 0.05% to –0.35%.

Portfolio Performance

During the first quarter, the Harbor Core Bond Fund (Institutional Class, “Fund”) returned 2.70%, slightly underperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index, which returned 2.78%.

Within credit, our underweight to and security selection within the Industrials sector contributed to performance. The Fund’s security selection within commercial mortgage-backed securities (“CMBS”) and asset-backed securities (“ABS”) aided relative performance. The Fund’s out-of-benchmark allocation to Small Business Administration (“SBA”) loans was also additive to excess returns. An underweight to Treasuries was the largest detractor from excess return during the period.

Contributors and Detractors

The greatest contributors to performance included Eversource Energy (ES), Foundry JV Holdco, Penske (PAG), and SBAs. The largest detractors from Fund performance included GATX (GATX), Xcel Energy (XEL), a Ginnie Mae pool, Berkshire Hathaway Energy, and Columbia Pipeline Group. Despite the underperformance, we continue to be constructive on these holdings and will continue to hold these securities.

Positioning

We strive to remain duration neutral and curve neutral to the benchmark.

Positive contributors to relative performance included our security section within SBAs and the Industrials sector. The Fund's out-of-benchmark allocation to SBAs aided relative returns.

Relative performance was also helped by our overweights to CMBS and ABS. Detractors from relative performance included security selection within the agency residential mortgage-backed securities (“RMBS”) sector and an out-of-index allocation to RMBS.

The Fund's security selection within the A and AAA credit buckets, as well as our overweight to AAAs, aided relative performance. On the other hand, our underweight to AA and overweight to BAA securities detracted from relative performance.

We invest exclusively in U.S. dollar-denominated fixed-income securities.

Buys & Sells

During the quarter, we purchased BX Trust’s BX 2025 VLT6 Z in the primary market at a spread of 1.50%. This purchase screened attractive relative to peers. At this level, we believe that we are being compensated for the tenant risk in an otherwise very solid data center deal.

We purchased Global Atlantic 5.5% 2032 in the secondary market at a spread of 1.30%. This purchase screened attractive relative to peers. We also bought Textron 5.5% 2035 in the secondary market at a spread of 1.02%. This purchase screened attractive relative to peers.

During the quarter, we sold the SBA’s SBAP 2024-25F 1. This was an opportunistic sale due to relative value. We sold the SBA’s SBAP 2024-25G 1. This was an opportunistic sale due to relative value.

We also sold GATX Corp 6.05% 2054. This was an opportunistic sale due to relative value.

Sector Overweights and Underweights

Entering the first quarter of 2025, the Fund was underweight Treasuries and overweight securitized, a theme that held true throughout the quarter, as the Fund ended the period with a deepened Treasury underweight of 20.99% — an increase of 1.90% — and a securitized overweight of 15.69% — an increase of 3.11%. The securitized overweight grew mainly due to increasing our overweight to non-agency CMBS by 0.46% and increasing our overweight to MBS by 2.70%. Our relative exposure to credit stayed flat, ending the quarter with an overweight of 2.33%. Within credit, we decreased our overweights to the Finance subsector by 0.27% and to the Utilities sector by 0.20%, while increasing our underweight to non-corporates by 0.07% and decreasing our underweights to Industrials by 0.54%.

Country Allocation

The Fund's country allocation relative to the benchmark did not change during the quarter. IR+M invests exclusively in U.S. dollar-denominated fixed-income securities. With that said, consistent with our bottom-up approach, we will opportunistically purchase Yankee issues when we believe they are attractive on a relative value basis.

During the quarter, the Fund's absolute weight of Yankees increased moderately to 8.74%.

Outlook

Although tariff headlines drove markets during the first quarter, investors received little certainty on their final form and ultimate impact on the economy. The administration’s capricious approach drove up risk asset volatility in March, with Fed messaging bringing the word “transitory” back into vogue as it relates to potential, negative short-term economic impacts. Lower Gross Domestic Product forecasts, coupled with declining consumer and business sentiment readings, reflect an economic backdrop that may start to show cracks under additional pressure. Consequently, we maintain a cautious approach to adding incremental risk to our Funds and rely on our duration-neutral philosophy to help withstand periods of increased interest rate volatility.
